testCases.name: test_case_0
testCases.input: How do I use the 'useActionState' hook to manage form state in React 19?
testCases.expectedOutput: useActionState is a Hook that allows you to update state based on the result of a form action. It takes a reducer function and initial state, returning an array with: 1. The current state, 2. A dispatch function to trigger the action, and 3. An 'isPending' boolean flag to track execution status. It replaces the older 'useFormState' hook.
retrievalContext:
By convention, functions that use async transitions are called "Actions". {/by-convention-functions-that-use-async-transitions-are-called-actions/}

Actions automatically manage submitting data for you:

Pending state: Actions provide a pending state that starts at the beginning of a request and automatically resets when the final state update is committed.

Optimistic updates: Actions support the new useOptimistic hook so you can show users instant feedback while the requests are submitting.

Error handling: Actions provide error handling so you can display Error Boundaries when a request fails, and revert optimistic updates to their original value automatically.

Forms: <form> elements now support passing functions to the action and formAction props. Passing functions to the action props use Actions by default and reset the form automatically after submission.

testCases.name: test_case_1
testCases.input: What is the new way to handle 'ref' as a prop in React 19 components?
testCases.actualOutput:
## Handling 'ref' as a Prop in React 19 Components According to the React documentation and my own knowledge (indicated below), there isn't a direct mention of changes to handling 'ref' as a prop in React 19 components in the provided documentation. However, I can provide information on the general best practices and potential changes. ### From the Documentation The provided documentation doesn't explicitly mention changes to handling 'refs' as props in React 19. However, it's essential to note that React 19 introduces several new features, such as Actions, use(), and useOptimistic. ### My Knowledge As of React 18 and continuing into React 19, the recommended way to handle 'refs' as props is to use the `forwardRef` function from React. **Before React 19 (and still applicable):** In the past, when you needed to pass a ref to a child component, you would typically use the `forwardRef` function to create a ref that can be passed as a prop. ```jsx import { forwardRef } from 'react'; const MyComponent = forwardRef((props, ref) => { // Use the ref as needed return
Hello World!
; }); ``` **New Way in React 19 (speculative, not directly from docs):** While not directly mentioned in the provided documentation, React 19's focus on improving server-side rendering, actions, and other features might lead to some adjustments in best practices. However, the fundamental way of handling refs using `forwardRef` is still expected to be applicable. It's essential to consult the official React documentation and upgrade guides for React 19 to ensure you're using the latest recommended approaches. ### Upgrade Guide and Official Documentation For the most up-to-date information on handling 'refs' as props in React 19, I recommend checking the official React documentation and the React 19 upgrade guide. ```bash npm install react@latest react-dom@latest ``` Or, if you're using yarn: ```bash yarn add react@latest react-dom@latest ``` Then, review the [React 19 Upgrade Guide](https://react.dev/blog/2024/04/25/react-19-upgrade-guide) and the [official React documentation](https://react.dev/) for the latest information on handling 'refs' as props.
testCases.expectedOutput: In React 19, 'ref' is now available as a standard prop in functional components. You no longer need to use the 'forwardRef' API to pass a ref to a child component; you can simply destructure 'ref' from the component's props.
retrievalContext:
title: Server Components

Server Components are a new type of Component that renders ahead of time, before bundling, in an environment separate from your client app or SSR server.

This separate environment is the "server" in React Server Components. Server Components can run once at build time on your CI server, or they can be run for each request using a web server.

How do I build support for Server Components? {/how-do-i-build-support-for-server-components/}

While React Server Components in React 19 are stable and will not break between minor versions, the underlying APIs used to implement a React Server Components bundler or framework do not follow semver and may break between minors in React 19.x.

To support React Server Components as a bundler or framework, we recommend pinning to a specific React version, or using the Canary release. We will continue working with bundlers and frameworks to stabilize the APIs used to implement React Server Components in the future.
